name: Kami of Mountains
description: The Kami of Mountains, known by various names depending on the specific mountain or region, represent the sacred and powerful essence of mountains in Japanese mythology. They are not singular entities, but rather a collective of spirits inhabiting and governing the mountains, often considered the most sacred places in Japan. These Kami are revered as providers of life-giving resources such as water, timber, and minerals, as well as protectors of the mountain's flora and fauna. Their power is often associated with the weather, particularly storms and landslides, reflecting the unpredictable nature of the mountains themselves. Offerings are traditionally made to appease and honor these Kami, ensuring good fortune and safety for those who traverse the mountains. They are often depicted as wise, ancient beings with a deep connection to the natural world, embodying both the beauty and the potential danger of the mountainous landscape. The worship of mountain Kami is deeply intertwined with Shinto practices, where mountains are viewed as gateways to the spirit world and places of spiritual pilgrimage. Their influence extends to the valleys below, impacting agriculture and the overall well-being of the community. Different mountains may have distinct Kami with unique personalities and legends, reflecting the diverse landscapes and cultural significance of each peak. The Kami of Mountains are crucial figures in maintaining harmony between humans and nature, representing the enduring power and reverence for the natural world in Japanese culture. Their role is to protect the mountain's ecosystem and to ensure that humans respect the mountains' power. Disrespecting the mountains can bring forth their wrath, resulting in natural disasters.
subtitle: Guardians of Peaks and Sacred Places

keyMyths.title: Birth of the Mountain Kami
keyMyths.description: Legend tells of the first mountain Kami arising from the primal chaos, solidifying the earth and shaping the landscape. This Kami provided stability and a foundation for other Kami and humans to inhabit.
keyMyths.source: Folk Tales of the Japanese Alps

keyMyths.title: The Wrath of the Mountain
keyMyths.description: A village angered the mountain Kami by cutting down too many trees, leading to a devastating landslide. The villagers then appeased the Kami with offerings, and the mountain eventually healed itself.

keyMyths.title: Guidance Through the Mountains
keyMyths.description: A lost traveler prayed to the mountain Kami and was guided to safety by a white deer, a sacred animal associated with the deity. The deer led the traveler back to the village.
